coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite photocatalyst from waste plant soot for efficient U(VI) removal: Mechanistic insights

coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal U (VI) wastewater is a difficult problem in water treatment because U (VI) readily dissolves in water and exhibits significant radiotoxicity and chemical toxicity. In the current work, the photocatalytic elimination of U (VI) is realized through the application of the Z-scheme 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 photocatalytic system. The system demonstrates high removal efficiency, efficient collection ability of natural visible light and considerable upconversion performance. Employing uranyl nitrate hexahydrate (N2O8U·6H2O) as the simulated pollutant of heavy metal U (VI), the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 achieved a removal rate of 98.84 % in 30 min under the condition of pH = 3, and the reaction rate constant (k) was 0.09972 min−1. Cycling stability tests demonstrated consistent performance of the photocatalyst over four repeated cycles, indicating its potential practical application in heavy metal pollution remediation. The investigation into the boosted photocatalytic activity delved into the analysis of optical properties, including the ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectrum (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Additionally, electrochemical tests, such as Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and photocurrent response, were employed to unravel the underlying factors contributing to this enhancement. Furthermore, we proposed a credible catalytic mechanism for the photocatalytic system. The Z-scheme photocatalytic model serves to enhance the conveyance of photogenerated carriers, thereby mitigating the electron-hole recombination rate within the photocatalyst. This work not only provides ideas and strategies for the construction of other multi-functional composite photocatalysts, but also pioneers a novel approach for addressing heavy metal pollution.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite photocatalyst from waste plant soot for efficient U(VI) removal: Mechanistic insights

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal U (VI) wastewater is a difficult problem in water treatment because U (VI) readily dissolves in water and exhibits significant radiotoxicity and chemical toxicity. In the current work, the photocatalytic elimination of U (VI) is realized through the application of the Z-scheme 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 photocatalytic system. The system demonstrates high removal efficiency, efficient collection ability of natural visible light and considerable upconversion performance. Employing uranyl nitrate hexahydrate (N2O8U·6H2O) as the simulated pollutant of heavy metal U (VI), the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 achieved a removal rate of 98.84 % in 30 min under the condition of pH = 3, and the reaction rate constant (k) was 0.09972 min−1. Cycling stability tests demonstrated consistent performance of the photocatalyst over four repeated cycles, indicating its potential practical application in heavy metal pollution remediation. The investigation into the boosted photocatalytic activity delved into the analysis of optical properties, including the ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectrum (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Additionally, electrochemical tests, such as Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and photocurrent response, were employed to unravel the underlying factors contributing to this enhancement. Furthermore, we proposed a credible catalytic mechanism for the photocatalytic system. The Z-scheme photocatalytic model serves to enhance the conveyance of photogenerated carriers, thereby mitigating the electron-hole recombination rate within the photocatalyst. This work not only provides ideas and strategies for the construction of other multi-functional composite photocatalysts, but also pioneers a novel approach for addressing heavy metal pollution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous acceleration of global economization and industrialization, the demand for energy around the world is growing rapidly. Fossil energy accounts for more than 80 % of the total global energy consumption[1]. Fossil energy such as coal, oil, and natural gas is nearing depletion. At the same time, in the process of consuming fossil energy, a large amount of harmful gases such as CO2 are emitted, causing environmental pollution, which in turn triggers the greenhouse effect[2], causing huge economic losses, and is extremely detrimental to the survival of humans and other organisms. For this reason, countries around the world have begun to devote themselves to finding alternatives to fossil energy. At present, nuclear energy is widely acknowledged as a prime alternative to fossil energy due to its superior energy density, enabling the generation of large-scale electricity without emitting greenhouse gases[3]. The burgeoning growth of the nuclear energy industry has led to a substantial surge in the demand for uranium, the primary source of nuclear fuel[4,5]. Currently, a large amount of U (VI) radioactive wastewater is produced. U (VI) is deposited in the organs and bones of the human body through the food chain, causing chemical and radiotoxicity humans[6–8]. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to remove uranium from the wastewater polluted by U (VI).

At present, the traditional methods to treat U (VI)-containing wastewater are adsorption, membrane separation, extraction, biological method and so on[9,10]. However, the above methods have some shortcomings, such as limited adsorption capacity, secondary pollution, complex process and high cost[11,12]. Photocatalysis has garnered significant attention owing to its environmental friendliness, high removal efficiency, easy operation, and cheap and readily available raw materials[13,14]. The photocatalytic method aids in the conversion of water-soluble U (VI) into insoluble U (IV) precipitates, thereby contributing to environmental protection goals. Therefore, the reduction of highly mobile U (VI) to less mobile U (IV) represents a promising solution. In recent years, there has been a growing focus on the photocatalytic treatment of wastewater containing U (VI), and many scholars have carried out research in this field. Chen et al.[15] fabricated a novel conjugated microporous polymer, TT-TPP, characterized by exceptional chemical stability and photochemical properties, which demonstrates efficacy in the photocatalytic reduction of U (VI) under highly acidic conditions. The photocatalytic removal of U (VI) utilizing SrTiO3/TiO2 [16] reached 81 ppm. ZIF-8/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was prepared[17] and used for photoreduction of U (VI) under various chemical conditions. The above photocatalysts can remove U (VI) from wastewater to some extent, but they are still some disadvantages such as harsh required conditions, low removal efficiency and complicated material preparation.

Typically, semiconductor materials such as TiO2 [18], ZnS[19] and CdS[20] are widely used as substrate materials for composite photocatalysts. These materials, unfortunately, possess a wide band gap, necessitating UV light sources with smaller wavelengths (<400 nm) to excite them and generate electron-hole pairs for initiating photocatalytic reactions[21]. For photocatalytic systems, the inability to absorb low-energy light sources will lead to low photocatalytic activity, which limits their practical applications in purifying the environment[22]. Hence, the challenges outlined above can be addressed through the advancement of photocatalytic systems incorporating superior photoactive materials. These materials should be responsive to low-energy excitation sources such as visible or near-infrared light and exhibit a high degree of photoelectric conversion efficiency. Among various photoactive nanomaterials, SnS2, as an important member of layered sulfides, has a narrow band gap, easily absorbs visible light with low energy, and has a high photogenerated carrier density[23]. Therefore, SnS2 can be considered as an ideal material for the field of photocatalysis.

However, in order to overcome the high electron-hole recombination rate and limited photocatalytic efficiency of single-phase SnS2 [24], further refinement of its intrinsic properties is critically needed. Two strategies are adopted. One is to perform elemental doping, which is a process of introducing foreign atoms into the host to enhance the physicochemical properties of the parent material. Doping affects the morphological, electrical, and optical properties of the parent material[25]. The second is the construction of heterojunctions, which are crystal interfaces formed by the combination of two or more semiconductor materials with different forbidden band widths. The promotion of charge transfer, the reduction of the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the enhancement of photocatalytic performance can be achieved through the formation of heterojunctions[26]. Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4), recognized as a metal-free semiconductor, has garnered considerable attention due to its exceptional optoelectronic properties, high thermal stability, chemical robustness, and responsiveness to visible light[27]. The unique energy band structure of g-C3N4 has been demonstrated to enable the feasibility of photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a novel category of “zero-dimensional” carbon-based nanomaterials. With inherent features such as exceptional electron transfer capabilities, upconversion photoluminescence and favorable biocompatibility, CQDs are considered as excellent candidates for interface regulation[18,28]. Plant soot belongs to environmental waste. Utilizing it to prepare CQDs can not only optimize the photocatalyst, but also achieve the purpose of waste recycling, which is in line with the concept of “green chemistry”[29] put forward by Professor P. T. Anatas of Yale University.

In this work, we introduce a pioneering method using waste plant soot to synthesize 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite photocatalysts, significantly advancing beyond the scope of conventional photocatalytic materials. Recent literature predominantly focuses on g-C3N4/SnS2 composites utilizing chemically synthesized CQDs[30,31], often overlooking the potential for eco-friendly, waste-derived CQDs. Our approach not only champions the principles of green chemistry but also substantially enhances photocatalytic activity under visible light, addressing a limitation frequently cited in existing research[32]. Additionally, our methodology tackles the prevalent issue of low charge carrier efficiency, a critical obstacle in the efficiency of photocatalytic systems[33]. By incorporating CQDs sourced from plant soot, we have not only broadened the light absorption spectrum but also enhanced charge separation. This innovative approach highlights the distinctiveness of our research and contributes to the advancement of sustainable photocatalytic technologies.

In this context, composite photocatalysts comprising g-C3N4/SnS2-Vx/CQDs with varying mass ratios were synthesized through a straightforward solvothermal method, leading to the successful achievement of efficient photocatalytic removal of U (VI). Moreover, ternary composites of V-doped SnS2 and plant soot CQDs loaded on layered g-C3N4 for photocatalytic removal of U (VI) have not been reported, and the factors affecting their optical activity are still unclear. Here, we successfully developed a facile synthesis method for composite photocatalysts and investigated the activity and stability of the composites for the reduction reaction of U (VI) in the aqueous phase. Additionally, we have comprehensively investigated the mechanism behind the heightened optical activity of the composite. The V-doping and heterojunction construction methods are used for g-C3N4, which significantly improves the conductivity of the base material, broadens its adsorption range, and also enhances its photoelectrochemical performance. The g-C3N4/SnS2-Vx/CQD1 proved to be a promising photocatalytic remover for U (VI) in radioactive wastewater, which provides ideas for the development of other more efficient and convenient g-C3N4-based nanocomposite photocatalysts, as well as the possibility of other heavy metals removal in environmental treatment.
Details are in the Supplementary Material.

Fig. 1 a is the SEM spectrum of SnS2, which is an ultra-thin hexagonal crystal with a particle size of approximately 500 nm. The morphological structure of SnS2 changed significantly after V doping in Fig. 1b, and the sheet structure of SnS2 was destroyed, transforming from the original hexagonal nanosheets with larger particle size to spherical nanoparticles with smaller particle size, about 20–40 nm in diameter. Fig. 1c shows the structure of g-C3N4, which is stacked nanolayers. In Fig. 1d, it is observed that the nanosheet-like g-C3N4 is loaded with numerous irregular SnS2-V3 nanospheres. However, the layered nanosheets are not completely covered by the SnS2-V3 nanospheres, leaving some areas exposed. Moving to Fig. 2 a, it is apparent that compared to Fig. 1d, there are no longer bare nanosheets visible. Instead, the nanosheets of g-C3N4 are now fully covered by smaller-sized nanospheres, suggesting successful loading of CQDs onto the surface of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3. The EDS mapping (Fig. 2b) and EDS spectrum (Fig. S2) reveal the elemental composition of the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite, demonstrating a uniform distribution of Sn, V, O, S, C, and N elements on the surface of the composite. The TEM and HRTEM images of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 are shown in Fig. 2c-d. On the surface of the g-C3N4, a large number of SnS2-V3 and CQDs are observed from Fig. 2c. The lattice spacings of the SnS2 (001) plane, CQDs (100) plane and g-C3N4 (002) plane measured from Fig. 2d are 0.59, 0.22 and 0.32 nm[34–36], respectively, indicating that the material has good crystallinity and the successful preparation of the composite.

The crystal structures and compositions of pure SnS2 hexagonal crystals, V-doped SnS2 nanoparticles, flake-like g-C3N4 and 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 were investigated by XRD analysis. In Fig. 3 a, the peaks observed at 14.9° and 27.4° in the diffraction pattern can be attributed to the (100) and (002) diffraction planes of g-C3N4 [JCPD87-1526], respectively. And the signals at 15.1°, 28.3°, 32.1°, 41.9°, 50.0°, 52.5°, 55.0°, 60.7°, 67.3° and 70.5° could be attributed to the (001), (100), (101), (102), (110), (111), (103), (201), (202) and (113) cubic planes of the crystal structure of SnS2 [JCPD23-0677]. For the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 composite, the signals at 14.9° and 15.1° are too close to be distinguished, and the other peak signals match well with the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 and SnS2. No diffraction peak of CQDs was observed in the XRD pattern of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite photocatalyst. The reason is that the crystal structure of the binary heterojunction remains unaffected by the incorporation of a small quantity of CQDs. The concentration of CQDs in nanocomposites is too low, and the diffraction intensity is too small to be observed. In addition, no characteristic peaks of any other impurities were detected, indicating that 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 was pure with good crystallinity.

To delve deeper into the atomic structure of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 heterojunction, the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b) of the prepared catalysts were analyzed. First, the broad-band peak located at 3480–3350 cm−1 can be associated with the O–H stretching vibration of H2O present on the material surface[37]. Second, the peak at 3230–3070 cm−1 is the N–H stretching vibration mode, while a series of peaks spanning from 1650 cm−1 to 1200 cm−1 represent typical stretching vibrations of C = O, C-N heterocycles and N–H bending vibration modes[38,39]. Moreover, the existence of oxygenated functional groups is confirmed by the peak at around 1130 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of C-O[38]. Additionally, the distinctive peak at 805 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibrational mode of the s-triazine ring[40]. In the case of SnS2 nanosheets, the broadband peak at 545 cm−1 arises from the vibration of the Sn-S band. Upon the incorporation of SnS2 nanosheets, the characteristic peak of the s-triazine ring vibration in the composite shifted from 805 to 812 cm−1. This shift implies a potential interaction between the N of g-C3N4 and the Sn of SnS2 [41]. FT-IR results indicated that SnS2-V3 and plant soot CQDs were successfully modified on the g-C3N4 surface.

The surface structure and pore size of g-C3N4, SnS2-V3 and the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite were investigated by employing the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller theorem. In Fig. 3c, the average pore sizes of SnS2-V3, g-C3N4 and the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 are 3.10, 24.58 and 5.66 cm3/g, respectively, indicating that the above catalysts have good mesoporous structure. Fig. 3d shows the adsorption–desorption isotherms of photocatalysts. According to the shape of their mesoporous hysteresis rings, these curves belong to type IV isotherms[42]. The BET specific surface area for SnS2-V3 is 188.19 m2/g, while for g-C3N4, it is 5.50 m2/g. The BET specific surface area witnessed an increase to 92.94 m2/g when SnS2-V3 and CQDs were incorporated onto the g-C3N4 surface in the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite. The expanded surface area and preserved porous structure are conducive to the adsorption of heavy metal ions. This, in turn, facilitates the provision of additional surface active centers in photocatalytic reactions.

Furthermore, the XPS spectra were utilized to characterize the surface chemical composition and bonding configuration of the composites. (Fig. 4 ). From the spectra, it was observed that C, N, O, S, Sn and V elements were simultaneously present in the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1. From the high resolution spectra of C 1 s in Fig. 4a reveal that the four peaks of C are situated at 284.7, 285.9, 286.5 and 288.2 eV, These peaks correspond to the C–C of g-C3N4, C-N, N-C = N and C = O/C-O in the s-triazine ring[40]. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups verified that the plant soot CQDs were successfully loaded on the composite surface. The N 1 s spectrum in 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 (Fig. 4b) consists of four split signals of 398.6, 399.8, 401.2 and 401.6 eV, which can be indexed as C-N = C bonds, N-(C)3 groups, Sn-N bonds and C = N bonds[43,44]. Presenting two principal peaks centered at 530.1 and 531.3 eV, respectively, the high-resolution O 1 s spectra in Fig. 4c, which can be associated with the binding energy of C = O and C-O-O/C-OH[45]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the S 2p1/2 peaks of the composite catalyst appear at 164.2 and 162.8 eV, respectively, and the S 2p3/2 peaks split into two signals of 163.7 and 161.7 eV. The peaks of 162.8 and 161.7 eV can be attributed to S-Sn bonds, confirming the existence of SnS2 [46]. Meanwhile, the signals at 164.2 and 163.7 eV are consistent with the binding energy of S-C, proving the successful preparation of the composite. In Fig. 4e, two characteristic peaks are observed at 486.3 and 494.8 eV in the Sn 3d high-resolution spectra, corresponding to the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2, respectively. These peaks are indicative of the presence of Sn-S bonds. In addition, two weak peaks at 486.6 and 495.7 eV can be clearly observed, and the signal here may be related to the existence of Sn-C bonds, further verifying the introduction of SnS2-V3 and CQDs[34]. Fig. 4f is the XPS spectrum of V 2p. The peaks at 517.2 and 516.4 eV belong to V5+ and V4+ [47], which proves that the V element was successfully doped into the target compound. The above results show that V elements are successfully doped into the structure of SnS2, and then the surface of g-C3N4 is well-coated with SnS2-V3 and CQDs, forming stable chemical bonds on the composite surface, resulting in a smoother charge transport process on its surface.

To comprehensively evaluate the photocatalytic materials developed in this study, both XPS and EDS were used to quantify elemental compositions. Detailed methodologies and corresponding data can be found in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S2 and S3). The atomic percentages obtained from the XPS analysis demonstrate significant surface enrichment of Sn and V, which is crucial for the observed photocatalytic activity. In contrast, the EDS data provide a broader compositional view, confirming the overall stoichiometry. The differences between these datasets highlight the importance of surface phenomena in the photocatalytic process.

The photocatalytic reduction of U (VI) was employed to assess the catalytic performance of the prepared photocatalysts. (Fig. 5 ). As depicted in Fig. 5a, the undoped V-element SnS2 had almost no catalytic effect within 90 min, with a U (VI) reduction rate of only about 4.10 %. When the doping of element V was increased, the U (VI) removal rate increased to 74.92 % (90 min). The reason for this phenomenon may be that the doping of element V causes SnS2 to produce absorption spectra in the UV–Vis-NIR range, which shows a new band gap characteristic. The removal efficiency of g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 is shown in Fig. 5b, in which 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 has the highest photocatalytic efficiency, reaching a removal rate of 93.50 % within 90 min, indicating that formation of heterojunction can improve the photoinduced carrier transport efficiency, thus improving the photocatalytic performance. To further investigate the impact of plant soot CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3, we examined the influence of varying CQDs ratios on the reduction efficiency of the ternary composite. In Fig. 5c, the photocatalytic reduction efficiency of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 is the highest, and the addition amount of CQDs is 1 mL. Not only the removal rate is increased from 93.50 % to 98.84 %, but also the reaction time is greatly shortened, from the original 90 min to 30 min. After being modified with CQDs, the CQDs on the material surface can upconvert long-wavelength light to excite photocatalysts to increase the number of electron-hole pairs generated. Fig. 5d is the result of the control test, under conditions with no catalyst and no light, the reduction of U (VI) was minimal, illustrating the effectiveness of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1. To explore the effect of pH, the initial pH of U (VI) aqueous solution was adjusted to 1–4 using HCl and NaOH solutions. As shown in Fig. 5e, when the pH was 1 and 2, the U (VI) concentration did not decrease significantly within 30 min, which may be due to the increased solubility of U (IV) and less adsorption of U (VI) on the catalyst surface, so the local concentration is too low, which is very unfavorable for the reduction. Increasing the pH to 3 resulted in a substantial enhancement in the reduction efficiency, highlighting the evident reliance of the photocatalytic process on the pH. At pH = 3, UO2 2+ species dominate and exhibit higher solubility, making them easier to remove compared to UO2(HA)+ ions[48]. However, when the pH continued to increase to 4, the removal efficiency decreased instead, which may be because [UO2HA]+ became the main form of U (VI) in the solution[49], which eventually led to a decrease in the removal rate. The photocatalytic reduction kinetics (k) is calculated using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Ln (Ct/C0) = kt)[50], and the fitting curve is shown in Fig. S4. Here, C0 represents the initial U (VI) concentration, Ct is the U (VI) concentration when t (min) is irradiated, and k represents the pseudo-first-order rate constant. As a result, the rate constant of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 reaction under the best photocatalytic reaction condition is 0.09972 min−1, which is higher than that of most of the published photocatalytic U (VI)[51,52]. In the comparative study presented in Table 1 , we analyzed recent research on the photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). The material developed in this study demonstrated a higher removal efficiency compared to traditional photocatalysts (such as TiO2 and ZnO) and other recently reported materials. Furthermore, it significantly shortened the reaction time, reducing it by 4 to 6 times, thereby saving time and cost. These results underscore the uniqueness and high efficiency of the material, proving its potential for advanced environmental cleanup applications where rapid and effective removal of contaminants is crucial.

As the cycling stability of photocatalyst has important potential application value for the removal of heavy metal pollutants, four repeated experiments were carried out (Fig. 5f). The photocatalytic removal rate remained stable in the first cycle (98.84 %), the second cycle (96.82 %), the third cycle (86.28 %) and the fourth cycle (82.65 %), respectively. It is showed that the catalyst can be reused and has the possibility of practical application in the remediation of heavy metal pollution. To understand how the structure, morphology, and surface characteristics of composite catalysts affect their performance and stability during the photocatalytic process, a detailed analysis of the FT-IR before and after the use of the photocatalyst was conducted. Initially, specific functional groups in the FT-IR spectra of the composite photocatalyst were discussed in detail. The FT-IR spectra of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 after photocatalysis, as shown in Supplementary Material Fig. S5, identified peaks at 1498 and 1611 cm−1, attributed to C-N and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively. These functional groups play a crucial role in photocatalytic activity as they are involved in the electron transfer processes required for the photocatalytic reduction of U(VI)[62]. The FT-IR analysis before and after photocatalysis showed almost no change in peak positions, indicating that the main functional groups remain chemically stable throughout the photocatalytic process. Although the positions of the functional groups remained stable, a weakening in peak intensity was observed after photocatalysis, suggesting that the density of U(VI) on the surface may have increased, potentially enhancing the interaction with U(VI). This interaction is crucial for the photocatalytic process, as it promotes the adsorption and reduction of U(VI) on the catalyst surface, thereby enhancing the overall reaction kinetics[63]. The FT-IR analysis of the regenerated catalyst (Fig. S5) shows that the peak intensity of the main functional groups is obviously enhanced, indicating that the interaction between the functional groups and U (VI) disappears and U (VI) is desorbed successfully from the surface of the catalyst.

To comprehensively assess the practical applicability of photocatalysts, a thorough analysis of their potential degradation mechanisms reveals several challenges affecting their efficiency and durability. Over time, the active sites of photocatalysts may become deactivated due to the accumulation of reaction intermediates or external impurity poisoning. Concurrently, physical or chemical alterations during extended use can diminish the catalyst's effective surface area, critical for its reactivity[64]. These surface changes are often accompanied by structural transformations in the photocatalyst’s crystal structure or phase transitions, which can further impede its activity[65]. Moreover, the fouling of the catalyst surface by pollutants or by-products obstructs light absorption and disrupts reaction kinetics. Additionally, certain photocatalytic by-products may interact detrimentally with the catalyst, hastening its degradation or deactivation[66]. From the experimental results above, it can be concluded that the photocatalyst developed in this study is relatively robust. It is projected to withstand long-term operational stress while maintaining high catalytic performance. This finding highlights the potential of the developed material for sustainable applications in environmental remediation, where stability and efficiency are crucial.

An array of optical property characterizations were conducted to clarify the role of the composite material in improving the efficiency of photocatalytic reduction for U (VI). It is expected that the detailed analysis can provide a certain reference value for the development of other composite photocatalysts. First, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were employed to examine the optical absorption of the catalysts (Fig. 6 a). A red-shift in the absorption peak of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 is observed, shifting from 447 nm to 554 nm in comparison to single g-C3N4 nanoparticles. This shift indicates that g-C3N4 loaded with SnS2-V3 nanoparticles possesses an increased visible light absorption and narrower band gap. The absorption cut-off edge of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 shifted to 586 nm in the long-wavelength direction, and its absorption intensity was higher compared to 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3. The broadening of the visible absorption spectrum can be attributed to the upconversion capability of CQDs[67]. In order to study the recombination and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, the PL spectra are shown in Fig. 6b. Pure g-C3N4 has the highest emissivity, which is manifested by high-speed electron-hole recombination, which proves that the single-phase g-C3N4 nanosheet has poor photocatalytic ability. Following the introduction of SnS2-V3 nanoparticles and CQDs onto the g-C3N4 surface, there was a substantial decrease in the emissivity of the prepared samples. The order of fluorescence intensity is g-C3N4 > SnS2-V3 > 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 > 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1. The PL emission peak intensity of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 is the lowest, indicating that the ternary composite exhibits the highest efficiency in separating photogenerated e--h+, thus possessing the highest photocatalytic potential. The properties of transport and separation of electron-hole were examined through EIS and transient photocurrent response. A more robust charge transfer ability is reflected in the EIS curve by a smaller arc radius[68]. As shown in Fig. 6c, compared with other photocatalysts, 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 exhibits a rather small arc radius, which indicates that the ternary composite photocatalyst will show the utmost photocatalytic ability. The presence of S-C bonds between g-C3N4 and SnS2-V3 facilitates the efficient overcoming of interfacial transport resistance by photogenerated carriers in SnS2-V3, allowing them to swiftly transfer to g-C3N4 [69]. Through the switching test under the irradiation of xenon lamp, Fig. 6d shows the photocurrent response spectrum of the prepared material, and the obvious photocurrent response of the synthesized material is observed. When the xenon lamp light source was turned off, the current intensity quickly returned to zero, indicating that the photoresponse ability of the sample has good reproducibility. Meanwhile, the photocurrent response intensity of the ternary heterojunction is approximately 9 times greater than that of g-C3N4. The larger the photocurrent, the higher the separation efficiency of e--h+ [70], so the photoinduced e--h+ of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 can be separated more efficiently. The results of EIS are consistent with the transient photocurrent response, further indicating that the heterojunction of the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 is conducive to charge separation and photogenerated carrier migration, thereby improving the photocurrent response intensity and the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy level structure of the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the energy band structure of the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1, the flat-band potentials of the prepared samples were determined by M−S plots. As shown in Fig. 6e, the slopes of the linear portions of the M−S profiles are all positive[71], and it can be determined that both g-C3N4 and SnS2-V3 are typical n-type semiconductors. Moreover, the computed results reveal that g-C3N4 has a flat-band potential of −1.00 eV, while SnS2-V3 has a flat-band potential of −0.26 eV. It has been reported in the literature that n-type semiconductors generally exhibit a conduction band (CB) approximately −0.1 eV lower than the flat-band potentials[72], so it is deduced that the CB of g-C3N4 and SnS2-V3 are −1.10 and −0.36 eV, respectively. In Fig. 6f, the estimated bandgaps are obtained from the linear part of the Tauc plots. The bandgaps of g-C3N4 and SnS2-V3 are 2.87 and 2.19 eV, respectively. Compared with the single-phase material, the bandgap of 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3 and 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 is reduced, which further proves that the composite material's ability to absorb visible light has been improved. Finally, according to the CB and bandgap width of the photocatalyst, the energy level structure of the composite material can be roughly calculated, which provides a basis for the analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism.

For investigating the possible mechanism of photocatalysis in this work, EPR and UPS characterization were performed on the prepared samples. The Fermi energy levels (Ef) of the samples g-C3N4 and SnS2-V3 were obtained as 0.33 and 0.68 eV, respectively, by UPS tests (Fig. 7 a-b). Since the higher Ef of SnS2-V3 compared to g-C3N4, the electrons generated in the CB of SnS2-V3 can transfer to the valence band (VB) of g-C3N4, and their Ef will be close to each other until their Ef is the same, and eventually a built-in electric field will be formed[73]. To understand the participation of active radical species during the photocatalytic process, the ESR analysis of the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 was performed. Illustrated in Fig. 7c, the distinctive signal peaks of 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 prove the generation of •O2 – and •OH radicals in the photocatalytic process[74]. Considering the characterization, photoelectrochemical outcomes, and the involvement of active radicals, it is proposed that the possible mechanism of U (VI) removal by the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 under visible light irradiation is an indirect Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism, as shown in Fig. 8 . When exposed to visible light, e- move from VB of SnS2-V3 and g-C3N4 to the CB, generating h+ on the VB. The electron sacrificial agent (methanol) has excellent electron donating properties[75], and its ability to effectively remove h+ minimizes the electron-hole recombination rate, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, plant soot CQDs, functioning as electron acceptors and donors, expedite the transfer of electrons from the CB of SnS2-V3 to the VB of g-C3N4 [76]. The spatial separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs occurs, effectively limiting carrier recombination. Therefore, the e- on the CB of g-C3N4 and the h+ on the VB of SnS2-V3 can maintain a strong redox property, enhancing the catalytic ability of the composite photocatalyst. In addition, CQDs not only enhance visible light and even near-infrared light harvesting but also act as electron acceptors and donors, facilitating the transfer of photogenerated charges. Under near-infrared light, e- in the π orbital of CQDs transition to the high-energy π* orbital, emitting visible light as the excited electrons return to the low-energy σ orbital through radiative relaxation[77]. On the one hand, U (VI) can be directly reduced by photoelectrons. On the other hand, the reduction of U (VI) occurs as •O2 – reacts with dissolved oxygen, forming U (IV). Meanwhile, the electron sacrificial agent reacts with photogenerated holes to degrade into CO2 and water. The specific photocatalytic reduction pathway of U (VI) is in equations (1)-(5).
(1) 2 %g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 + hv → e-+h+
(2) O2 + e-→•O2 –
(3) U(VI) + 2e-/2•O2 –→U(IV)
(4) H2O + h+→•OH
(5) CH3OH + h++•OH → CO2 + H2O

In summary, using waste plant soot as raw material, the Z-scheme 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 composite photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by solvothermal method and sonochemical method, and applied to the photocatalytic removal of U (VI). This work can not only efficiently remove the heavy metal hexavalent uranium, but also achieve the purpose of waste utilization and practice the concept of green chemistry. Under visible light irradiation, using uranyl nitrate solution as a simulated pollutant, the photocatalytic performance experiments were conducted to explore the impact of SnS2-V3 and CQDs loading, pH value and other conditions on reduction efficiency. Optimal experimental conditions led to a removal rate exceeding 90 % for U (VI) by the 2 % g-C3N4/SnS2-V3/CQD1 in just 30 min. Conducting four cycles of experiments to assess the stability of the composite photocatalyst revealed only a slight decrease in the efficiency of photocatalytic removal of U (VI). This suggests that the prepared composite photocatalyst exhibits good cycle stability and holds great potential for practical applications. Through a series of characterization and experiments, the reason and mechanism of photoactivity enhancement of composite photocatalyst were discussed in detail. This work not only presents a novel approach for remediating hexavalent uranium pollution in groundwater, but also provides ideas for the recovery and utilization of other wastes.
